title: An AI agent coding skeptic tries AI agent coding, in excessive detail
description: Max Woolf describes his journey from skepticism to enthusiasm about AI coding agents. Starting with simple YouTube metadata scrapers, he progressed to porting Python's scikit-learn to Rust. Key insight: 'Opus 4.5 (and the models that came after it) are an order of magnitude better than coding LLMs released just months before it' - highlighting the dramatic improvement in coding agent capabilities around November 2025.
author: Max Woolf (covered by Simon Willison)

title: Free Claude Max for (large project) open source maintainers
description: Anthropic now offers their $200/month Claude Max 20x plan for free to open source maintainers for six months. Criteria: primary maintainer of public repo with 5,000+ GitHub stars OR 1M+ monthly NPM downloads, with commits/releases/PR reviews in the last 3 months. Applications reviewed on rolling basis, up to 10,000 contributors accepted.
author: Simon Willison

title: Unicode Explorer using binary search over fetch() HTTP range requests
description: Simon built a prototype tool from his phone demonstrating HTTP range requests and binary search. The tool looks up Unicode codepoints by binary searching through MBs of metadata, fetching only 256-byte records per step. Key technical insight: Range request tricks aren't compatible with HTTP compression due to byte offset calculations.
author: Simon Willison

title: Agentic Engineering Patterns: Hoard things you know how to do
description: A key pattern for working productively with coding agents: understanding what's possible and having rough ideas of how to accomplish things. The skill in building software is knowing what questions to ask - from 'Can a web page run OCR in JavaScript?' to 'Can we process 100GB JSON in Python without loading it all into memory?'
author: Simon Willison

title: Google API Keys Weren't Secrets. But then Gemini Changed the Rules.
description: Critical security issue: Google Maps API keys (designed to be public in web pages) can access Gemini API endpoints when Gemini is enabled on the same project. This is a privilege escalation - keys transform from public identifiers to secret credentials without warning. Truffle Security found 2,863 API keys in Nov 2025 Common Crawl that could access Gemini, including some from Google themselves.
author: Simon Willison

title: Coding agents didn't work before December, basically work since - Andrej Karpathy
description: Andrej Karpathy quote: 'It is hard to communicate how much programming has changed due to AI in the last 2 months: not gradually and over time in the progress as usual way, but specifically this last December. Coding agents basically didn't work before December and basically work since - the models have significantly higher quality, long-term coherence and tenacity.'
author: Andrej Karpathy (quoted by Simon Willison)

title: Claude Code Remote Control
description: New Claude Code feature: run 'remote control' session on computer and use Claude Code for web interfaces (web, iOS, desktop) to send prompts. Current limitations: only one session at a time, doesn't support --dangerously-skip-permissions flag, some 500 API errors. Anthropic also announced scheduled tasks in Cowork, but with limitation that tasks only run while computer is awake and Claude Desktop app is open.
author: Simon Willison

title: I vibe coded my dream macOS presentation app
description: Simon vibe coded a custom macOS presentation app the night before his talk at Social Science FOO Camp. The talk: 'The State of LLMs, February 2026 edition' with subtitle 'It's all changed since November!'. Includes quote from Kellan Elliott-McCrea about people feeling loss at AI's impact on coding careers: 'Code has always been the easy part.'
author: Simon Willison

title: Agentic Engineering Patterns: Linear walkthroughs
description: Pattern for using coding agents to get structured walkthroughs of codebases - whether existing code you're learning, your own forgotten code, or vibe-coded code you need to understand. Frontier models with right agent harness can construct detailed walkthroughs to help understand how code works.
author: Simon Willison

title: Ladybird adopts Rust, with help from AI
description: Andreas Kling reports using Claude Code and Codex to port Ladybird's JavaScript engine (LibJS) from C++ to Rust. Human-directed process with hundreds of small prompts, not autonomous. Result: 25,000 lines of Rust in ~2 weeks (vs multiple months by hand). Every AST and bytecode output is byte-for-byte identical to C++ version - zero regressions. Success enabled by test262 conformance testing suite.
author: Andreas Kling (covered by Simon Willison)

title: Agentic Engineering Patterns: Writing code is cheap now
description: The biggest challenge in adopting agentic engineering: getting comfortable with the fact that writing code is cheap now. Traditional engineering habits are built around code being expensive - we spend time designing, estimating, and planning. With AI, producing hundreds of lines of clean, tested code is suddenly trivial. This requires rethinking everything from how we evaluate features to how we approach refactoring.
author: Simon Willison

title: tldraw considers moving tests to closed source repo (later revealed as joke)
description: tldraw filed an issue about moving their test suite to private repo, in response to Cloudflare porting Next.js to Vite in a week using AI. The concern: comprehensive test suites are enough to build completely fresh implementations of open source libraries from scratch. Update: The issue was a joke. tldraw clarified that moving tests would complicate development, speed is more important than ever, and 'the value is in the ability to produce new and good product decisions for users, however you choose to create the code.'
author: Simon Willison
